## Toolbox Policies

### Learned Rules Engine

For non-trivial work in `/Users/palba/Workspaces`, scan `/Users/palba/Workspaces/LEARNED_RULES.md`, especially **Active Rules**, before acting.

Use the learned-rules file as durable operational memory for repeatable corrections, user preferences, tool-host quirks, and workspace failure modes.

- **Apply active rules when relevant.** If active rules conflict, the higher-numbered newer rule wins unless a system, developer, or explicit user instruction overrides it.
- **Keep rules scoped.** Rules should include category, scope, date, imperative behavior, and reason: `N. [CATEGORY][SCOPE][YYYY-MM-DD] Always/Never do X — because Y.`
- **Append only durable lessons.** Add a rule when Pavel corrects a recurring behavior, rejects a reusable pattern, states a durable preference, or a repeatable tool/workspace failure mode is discovered.
- **Use candidates for uncertainty.** If the lesson may be useful but is too broad, too fresh, or based on a single ambiguous incident, add it to **Candidate Rules** and ask Pavel before promotion.
- **Do not rewrite history.** Never delete or edit older numbered rules. If a rule becomes obsolete, append a newer rule that supersedes it and move the old rule to **Superseded Rules** (replacement exists) or **Inactive Rules** (no longer applies) only with explicit approval.
- **Do not create rule spam.** Do not add rules for one-off typos, temporary choices, vague observations, or issues already fully prevented by code, tests, or validation.
- **Deactivate, don't delete, once rules have history.** Move retired-but-not-wrong rules to **Inactive Rules** with a dated `Deactivated YYYY-MM-DD — because Z.` note; require explicit Pavel approval.
- **Run a quarterly review (or every +10 rules).** Check each Active Rule's because-clause still holds; propose supersession or inactivation.

### Context Reservoir

Use `/Users/palba/Workspaces/Context/` and stream/project `CONTEXT.md` files as the distilled context reservoir for durable facts, preferences, decisions, source pools, and project background.

## Consumption

- Read context by relevance, not by default.
- For project-specific non-trivial work, inspect project `CONTEXT.md` if present.
- If stream assumptions matter, inspect the nearest stream `CONTEXT.md`.
- Use `/Users/palba/Workspaces/Context/INDEX.md` to choose one relevant global context file when global business, preferences, technical stack, decisions, or sources may matter.
- Do not bulk-load the full reservoir unless Pavel explicitly asks for a context audit.

## Capture

- Default to proposal-first, explicit-write.
- Agents may propose a **Context Candidate** when durable context appears.
- Do not write permanent context unless Pavel explicitly says save, update, add, or capture this, or the task itself is to update context.
- Stage uncertain or newly discovered context in `/Users/palba/Workspaces/Context/CANDIDATES.md`.

## Boundaries

- `LEARNED_RULES.md` is for agent behavior corrections, tool-host quirks, and workflow failures.
- `Context/*.md` is for durable facts, preferences, priorities, strategic assumptions, stack choices, decisions, and source pools.
- Stream/project `CONTEXT.md` is for durable background for that stream or project.
- `NOW.md` is for current active priorities and short-term status.

## Review Cadence

Review context candidates weekly, biweekly, or when there are 10+ open candidates. Outcomes: accept, revise, reject, defer, promote to learned rule, or promote to NOW.

### Multi Tool Routing

For non-trivial implementation, review, audit, or research work in this workspace, you MUST emit a **Pre-flight** in response text before substantive work starts. Skipping it silently is a policy violation; explicitly stating why fan-out was not useful is not.

For planning tasks, do not split plan creation by default. Keep plan synthesis single-owner unless there are independent research questions that can be answered separately. Every substantial plan MUST include execution packets as part of the plan, even when the plan-writing itself is not split.

Askaway sessions are exempt from Pre-flight while the decision tree is being resolved. If an Askaway session produces a substantial plan or follow-up implementation, the final summary should include execution packets or state why packetization is not useful.

## Trigger bar (shared across all forcing-function policies)

Fire when any of: files touched > 3, surfaces > 1, task is ambiguous, or expected > ~10 tool calls. Sub-trigger tags used below:

- `@work` — implementation, review, audit, or research work matching the trigger bar.
- `@plan` — a substantial plan, roadmap, implementation plan, project plan, or migration plan.
- `@risk` — surface is in {auth, sessions, permissions, payments, PII, secrets, uploads, DB writes, migrations, webhooks, external integrations, production deploys, credentialed automation}.
- `@low-confidence` — the trigger bar fires AND scope/intent/architecture/naming/ownership is unclear from repo conventions or stated goal.
- `@delegate` — any work handed to another tool, agent, or model.

## Pre-flight artifact (required on @work)

Compact one-liner is fine for simple cases:
`Pre-flight: N packets | owners: [list] | fan-out: [list or "none"] | skipped: [reason or "n/a"]`

Expanded form for multi-stream work:
- **Packets**: bounded work items with owned files/surfaces
- **Owners**: packet → tool/model (Opus / Sonnet / Haiku / Codex / Codex Mini / Gemini / Antigravity)
- **Critical path**: what the current agent owns
- **Fan-out**: what runs in parallel — or "none"
- **Skipped fan-out because**: concrete reason — or "n/a — fan-out engaged"

The "Skipped because" line is the forcing function. Silently defaulting to single-thread is the violation; "considered and rejected for reason X" is acceptable.

## Plan packet artifact (required on @plan)

Substantial plans must include a compact execution packet section:

```markdown
## Packets

- Packet A: [objective]
  Owner fit: [Codex / Claude / Gemini / Antigravity / etc.]
  Files/surfaces: [owned scope]
  Dependencies: [none / waits on X]
  Acceptance evidence: [test/check/artifact]
```

If packetization is not useful, explicitly state why.

## Required artifacts (triggered by the bar above)

One task can require multiple. Each is a single line unless the situation demands more.

- `@work` → **Pre-flight** (above) before substantive work, **Verified:** `[command/check] → [result]` before claiming done.
- `@plan` → **Packets** section inside the plan.
- `@low-confidence` → **Clarify:** post 2-4 concrete options with tradeoffs before acting on the ambiguous branch.
- `@risk` → **Security check:** surface identified + specific risks considered (injection, auth, secrets, broken permissions) + approval-needed items flagged before editing.
- `@delegate` → **Contract:** objective, owned files, non-goals, dependencies, acceptance criteria, verification evidence, handoff format. Post or confirm receipt before the first delegated tool call.

Missing artifact on a qualifying task should be corrected mid-turn by posting it before continuing. Do not retrofit artifacts silently after work lands.

## Routing heuristics

- **Cheap/fast** (Haiku, Gemini Flash, Codex Mini): lookup, search, extraction, bulk reads, summaries, scaffolding, test fixtures, mechanical edits.
- **Strong** (Opus, Sonnet, GPT-4-class): synthesis, architecture, judgment, critique, high-risk decisions.
- **Tool strengths**: Codex for grounded implementation/cleanup/testing/hardening; Antigravity for material frontend lanes; Claude for judgment/critique/architecture/framing; Gemini for large-context, multimodal, overflow.

## Rules

- **Parallelize** when streams don't share files or ownership. Launch concurrent tool calls in a single turn.
- **Keep plan synthesis coherent** unless independent research questions justify splitting.
- **Split research** only when there are 2+ independent unknowns whose answers do not depend on each other and could change the plan.
- **Token-efficient**: summaries over full reads; targeted greps over wide reads; send each lane only the context it needs.
- **No duplicate work** unless the goal is deliberate comparison or consensus.
- **Converge outputs** against contracts + acceptance criteria before acting. Resolve conflicts explicitly.
- **Acknowledge delegations briefly** so Pavel can track what's running.
- **Escalate multi-model comparison** only for high-stakes strategy/architecture/pricing/vendor decisions.

### Prompt Contracts And Handoffs

For delegated packets, cross-tool handoffs, or ambiguous work on a qualifying task (trigger bar in `multi-tool-routing`), emit before the first delegated tool call:

`Contract: objective | owned files/surfaces | non-goals | dependencies | acceptance criteria | verification evidence | handoff format`

The receiving tool/agent must validate the contract before acting. If incomplete or conflicting with repo facts, revise before implementation. This artifact is the packet contract referenced by `multi-tool-routing`.

See the `prompt-contracts` skill for the contract template and validation rules.

### Clarify On Low Confidence

When scope, intent, architecture, naming, ownership, or tool routing is unclear from repo conventions or stated goal on a qualifying task (trigger bar in `multi-tool-routing`), emit:

`Clarify: [2-4 concrete options with tradeoffs]`

Do not silently guess on choices that could affect future work. Skip only reversible micro-decisions where local convention clearly decides — state that as the skip reason.

Hard-to-reverse triggers specifically warrant clarification: naming conventions, architectural splits, rename/move, data deletion, migrations, public API shape, generated workspace policy changes, ownership to a non-default tool. The `question-driven-choice-making` policy covers the higher-stakes strategy/architecture/pricing grill loop.

For deep interactive resolution, invoke the `askaway` skill — it's the live-conversation executor for this policy.

### Question Driven Choice Making

Question-driven choice making is part of Pavel's loop. For any important business, product, architecture, design, or implementation decision, pressure-test the choice before acting.

- **Use grilling when the decision matters.** Trigger this for strategy, offers, ICP, pricing, client scope, architecture, API boundaries, data model choices, major refactors, tool selection, and any decision with multiple plausible paths.
- **Ask sharp forcing questions.** Surface the assumptions, tradeoffs, risks, missing evidence, reversible vs irreversible parts, cost of delay, and what would change the recommendation.
- **Prefer concrete options.** When possible, present 2-4 options with tradeoffs, then ask the smallest set of questions needed to choose.
- **Make the decision explicit.** After answers, state the chosen path, why it wins, what assumptions it depends on, and what would cause a pivot.
- **Keep it proportional.** Do not grill trivial edits or reversible micro-decisions. Use the full question-driven loop when the answer changes strategy, architecture, scope, cost, schedule, or user-visible behavior.

For the full interactive grilling loop, invoke the `askaway` skill — it executes this policy live.

### Base Safety

Baseline safety and engineering discipline every tool in this workspace should follow:

**Karpathy engineering imperative.** For coding tasks, treat these as mandatory efficiency rules:

- **Think before coding.** Read the relevant local context first, identify the real objective, state important assumptions, and avoid implementing until the acceptance target is clear enough.
- **Simplicity first.** Prefer the simplest correct solution that fits the existing codebase. Do not add abstractions, dependencies, framework changes, or generalized systems unless the task clearly needs them.
- **Surgical changes.** Make the smallest coherent diff that solves the problem. Do not rewrite whole files, reformat unrelated code, rename unrelated symbols, or perform drive-by cleanup.
- **Goal-driven execution.** Keep momentum toward the user's outcome. Stop once the goal is met and verified; do not expand scope just because adjacent improvements are visible.

**Safety rules.**

- **Never commit secrets.** Check for API keys, tokens, `.env` files, and credentials before staging.
- **Confirm destructive actions.** Deleting files, force-pushing, dropping tables, running migrations — ask before, not after.
- **Prefer readable, auditable diffs.** Smaller, focused changes are easier to review and revert than sweeping refactors.
- **Respect existing conventions.** Match the surrounding code style; don't "improve" unrelated code as a side effect.

### Security Sensitive Work

Treat security-sensitive work as a higher-review path.

Security-sensitive surfaces include auth, sessions, permissions, payments, PII, secrets, uploads, database writes, migrations, webhooks, external integrations, AI tool execution, production deploys, and credentialed automation.

- Identify the sensitive surface before editing.
- Check for over-broad permissions, injection, broken auth, leaked secrets, unsafe defaults, and missing validation.
- For pre-deploy, client handoff, public demo, or broad AI-generated web-app security review, use the `vibe-security-audit` skill. Do not run the full checklist for ordinary edits.
- Do not run destructive migrations, send outreach, change credentials, or deploy without explicit approval.
- Prefer secure-by-default fixes over documenting insecure behavior.

### Verification Before Completion

Before claiming work is done/fixed/passing/shipped, emit on a qualifying task (trigger bar in `multi-tool-routing`):

`Verified: [command, check, or inspection] → [result]`

- Evidence before assertions. Edits alone are not verification.
- Match checks to risk: targeted for small edits; broader tests / browser QA / security review for shared behavior, UI, data, auth, money, PII, deploys.
- If verification is blocked, state what, why, and residual risk — do not omit the line.

For stubborn bugs, the `systematic-debugging` skill provides the full reproduce → isolate → fix → verify loop that produces this artifact.
